context: Items where Gemini passes disagreed. Review and classify.

items.text: Tesla had conducted the Colorado Springs experiments in eighteen ninety-nine.
items.gemini_reasoning: Nikola Tesla's experiments in Colorado Springs in 1899 are historically documented.

items.text: The cracks began to spread outward, moving through time and geography. We can look at the major anomalies of the twentieth century not as isolated mysteries, but as secondary symptoms of the Tesla Fracture.
items.gemini_reasoning: This establishes a core tenet of the documentary's lore: the idea of a 'Tesla Fracture' causing 20th-century anomalies.

items.text: He had pumped millions of volts into the earth's crust. He had created standing waves of energy that circled the globe, bouncing off the antipodes and returning to the source. He had proven that the earth itself could be used as a conductor.
items.gemini_reasoning: These describe Tesla's reported activities and claims during the Colorado Springs experiments.

items.text: He never spoke publicly about what he had measured in those final weeks.
items.gemini_reasoning: This highlights a deliberate unknown in the historical record, presented as an unresolved mystery by the narrator.

items.text: He dismantled the laboratory.
items.gemini_reasoning: Tesla dismantling his Colorado Springs laboratory is a verifiable event.

items.text: its tower dark, its promise unfulfilled.
items.gemini_reasoning: This uses personification and evocative language to describe Wardenclyffe.

items.text: He was no longer conducting experiments. He was writing letters to creditors and dreaming of projects that would never be funded.
items.gemini_reasoning: Describes Tesla's personal and professional circumstances and internal state at that time.

items.text: The seismograph data from the laboratory in London for the morning of June thirtieth registered something four minutes before the explosion: A harmonic. A pulse. A frequency that matched the resonant frequency Tesla had documented in Colorado Springs.
items.gemini_reasoning: The specific detail of a London seismograph registering a Tesla-resonant frequency pulse four minutes before Tunguska is presented as a fact within the narrative, but is not independently verifiable.

items.text: Had the standing waves finally decayed?
items.gemini_reasoning: This is a direct question posed by the narrator, deliberately left unanswered to build suspense and invite speculation.

items.text: Had nine years of energy, circling the globe, finally found a point of discharge?
items.gemini_reasoning: This is a direct question posed by the narrator, deliberately left unanswered to build suspense and invite speculation.

items.text: Was Tunguska the echo of Colorado Springs???the delayed consequence of forcing the earth to conduct electricity it was never designed to carry?
items.gemini_reasoning: This is a direct question posed by the narrator, deliberately left unanswered to build suspense and invite speculation.

items.text: Tunguska the echo of Colorado Springs
items.gemini_reasoning: Uses a metaphor to connect two distant events, suggesting a resonant consequence.

items.text: forcing the earth to conduct electricity it was never designed to carry
items.gemini_reasoning: The concept of the earth being 'designed' or not 'designed' to carry electricity in this context is part of the documentary's fictional premise.

items.text: The explosion dominates every account. The megatons. The flattened forest.
items.gemini_reasoning: These are common and verifiable descriptions of the Tunguska event's impact.

items.text: the silence before the explosion is rarely discussed.
items.gemini_reasoning: This reflects a perspective or observation about how historians or popular accounts focus on certain aspects over others.

items.text: It found a weak point.
items.gemini_reasoning: Personifies the 'crack' or 'fracture' as an entity capable of finding something.

items.text: The cracks began to spread outward, moving through time and geography. We can trace them. We can look at the major anomalies of the twentieth century not as isolated mysteries, but as secondary symptoms of the Tesla Fracture.
items.gemini_reasoning: This establishes a core lore concept: the 'Tesla Fracture' as the underlying cause for various historical anomalies, reinterpreting known events through the documentary's fictional premise.

items.text: Tunguska. June thirtieth, nineteen oh eight. Siberia. An explosion flattens eighty million trees over eight hundred square miles. The energy release is estimated at fifteen megatons. History tells us it was a meteor. A comet. A space rock that exploded in the atmosphere. But there was no impact crater. There were no meteorite fragments found for decades, and even then, they were microscopic.
items.gemini_reasoning: These are all independently verifiable facts and common historical interpretations and observations about the Tunguska event.

items.text: He stopped. He dismantled the laboratory.
items.gemini_reasoning: These are verifiable actions Tesla took regarding his Colorado Springs laboratory.

items.text: It echoes through the medium until the energy is absorbed.
items.gemini_reasoning: The use of 'echoes' provides a vivid, auditory metaphor for the lingering effects of the standing waves.

items.text: its promise unfulfilled.
items.gemini_reasoning: This is a subjective assessment of Wardenclyffe's historical outcome.

items.text: He was no longer conducting experiments. He was writing letters to creditors.
items.gemini_reasoning: These are verifiable historical details about Tesla's activities and financial situation.

items.text: dreaming of projects that would never be funded.

items.text: Pull the seismograph data from the laboratory in London for the morning of June thirtieth. The needle didn't just register a shockwave from Siberia. It registered something four minutes before the explosion. A harmonic. A pulse. A frequency that matched the resonant frequency Tesla had documented in Colorado Springs.
items.gemini_reasoning: While London seismographs would exist, the specific detail of a reading 'four minutes before' Tunguska, described as a 'harmonic' matching Tesla's resonant frequency, is a central piece of the documentary's invented lore to link the events.

items.text: The earth's magnetic field was buckling.
items.gemini_reasoning: The verb 'buckling' vividly describes the distortion of the magnetic field, appealing to sensory experience.

items.text: Was Tunguska the echo of Colorado Springs?
items.gemini_reasoning: This is a powerful auditory metaphor linking two events through a lingering, reverberating effect.

items.text: forcing the earth to conduct electricity it was never designed to carry?

items.text: found a weak point.
items.gemini_reasoning: Personification of the 'crack' as actively seeking and finding a vulnerable spot, adding dramatic effect.

items.text: Tesla created a 'fracture' or 'incision' in 1856, which was later widened by his 1899 experiments, forming a core element of the documentary's lore.
The Tesla Fracture is presented as the underlying cause or explanation for various major anomalies of the twentieth century, which are deemed 'secondary symptoms'.
Tesla's 1899 experiments in Colorado Springs created powerful standing waves of energy that circled the globe.
The standing waves created by Tesla's experiments are implied to have a detrimental, unnatural effect on the Earth's crust, forcing it to conduct electricity beyond its design.
The narrative explicitly questions whether the Tunguska event was a delayed consequence, an 'echo,' of Tesla's Colorado Springs experiments and the standing waves they created.
The 'crack' (Tesla Fracture) had been spreading through the Earth's crust for almost a decade, acting parasitically by exploiting a 'weak point' for discharge.

A seismograph in a London laboratory registered a 'harmonic' or 'pulse' matching Tesla's resonant frequency, four minutes before the Tunguska explosion, indicating it 'witnessed' an unusual precursor event.
